findings.reachability: Any caller of decrypt() or decryptKeyInfo() with attacker-supplied XML — the URI attribute of a RetrievalMethod element in the XML is read from the document and spliced into XPath expressions without any sanitization.
findings.justification: keyRetrievalMethodUri.substring(1) is derived from keyRetrievalMethod.getAttribute('URI') (attacker-controlled XML attribute) and concatenated directly into XPath strings on lines 246 and 273, allowing an attacker to break out of the single-quoted string and inject arbitrary XPath predicates or functions to alter which nodes are selected during decryption.

detection_pattern.section_name: XPath injection: dynamic string concatenation in xpath.select
detection_pattern.description: Matches XPath queries built by string concatenation with getAttribute() results; the safe alternative is parameterized XPath using variable bindings (the `$var` syntax in the xpath library) rather than string interpolation.

findings.reachability: Any attacker who can supply a CBC-encrypted blob whose last byte encodes a padding value outside the range [1, ivLength] will trigger this code path — reachable from decrypt() and decryptKeyInfo() with attacker-supplied ciphertext.
findings.justification: decryptWithAlgorithm() is a synchronous function that has no 'callback' parameter; line 325 calls callback(new Error('padding length invalid')) on invalid padding, which is a ReferenceError in the synchronous context. The ReferenceError propagates up uncaught and crashes the process instead of being returned to the caller, creating a denial-of-service vector. This also leaks a timing/error oracle for CBC-mode padding.

detection_pattern.section_name: Callback used out of scope in sync decryption function
detection_pattern.description: Catches synchronous decryption helper functions that call a 'callback' identifier not present in their parameter list, which will throw a ReferenceError on error paths and crash the Node.js process.

findings.reachability: Callers of encrypt() or encryptKeyInfo() who pass an options.pem value that is not a well-formed certificate — e.g., a PEM containing XML special characters or crafted content between the certificate headers — can inject arbitrary XML into the KeyInfo block.
findings.justification: In xmlenc.js line 29 encryptionPublicCert is built by concatenating pemToCert(options.pem.toString()) with literal XML tags, then in keyinfo.tpl.xml.js line 10 it is interpolated as ${encryptionPublicCert} without calling escapehtml(), while every other field in that template is escaped; a PEM body containing characters like < > & or an extra </X509Certificate> sequence breaks the XML document structure.

detection_pattern.section_name: XML injection: unescaped template variable containing certificate/XML data
detection_pattern.description: Finds template literal interpolations of variables with names suggesting HTML/XML/certificate content that are not wrapped in escapehtml(), indicating missing output encoding in XML generation.

findings.reachability: Any caller of encrypt() where encryptKeyInfo returns a keyInfo string that was constructed from attacker-influenced certificate data; the keyInfo string is spliced raw into the outer EncryptedData XML document.
findings.justification: In encrypted-key.tpl.xml.js line 6, ${keyInfo} is inserted without escapehtml() — unlike contentEncryptionMethod and encryptedContent on lines 5 and 8 which are escaped — so if the keyInfo string (itself output of keyinfo template) contains extra XML, it could inject elements into the EncryptedData document.

detection_pattern.section_name: XML injection: unescaped keyInfo in template
detection_pattern.description: Catches template literal interpolations of variables named keyInfo or similar XML/HTML identifiers that bypass escapehtml(), allowing injected XML if the variable content is not sanitized upstream.

findings.reachability: Any attacker who can submit ciphertext to a service that calls decrypt() and observe whether an error is returned versus success — the padding check on line 321-327 produces a distinguishable error for invalid padding versus valid padding.
findings.justification: For CBC-mode algorithms (aes-128-cbc, aes-256-cbc, des-ede3-cbc) the custom padding removal on lines 321-327 returns an error when the last byte value is outside [1, ivLength], creating a padding oracle that an attacker can exploit to decrypt arbitrary ciphertext by submitting chosen ciphertexts and observing the error/success distinction.

detection_pattern.section_name: Padding oracle: custom CBC padding validation with error leak
detection_pattern.description: Detects manual CBC padding removal code that throws or returns distinguishable errors for invalid padding bytes, which enables a padding oracle attack against decrypted ciphertext.
